data.title: Apple: Rolling Over Into Key Earnings
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL) heads into the big holiday quarter report with the stock finally rolling over. The tech giant still lacks an AI business, and the expected launch of the AI Siri comes at a big cost, not a new revenue stream. My investment thesis remains ultra-bearish on Apple trading at a premium valuation with no signs of strong growth ahead.

Costly AI

Apple investors have long seen AI as an opportunity for Apple to generate a revenue stream similar to the Google partnership on Internet search. Google is paying Apple upwards of $20 billion for exclusive search placement in iOS products, but the tech giant has to pay for generative AI tech.

The company has reportedly chosen to work with Gemini from Google (GOOGL) at a reported cost of $1 billion. The news suggests Anthropic (ANTHRO) wanted $2+ billion over multiple years for working with Siri.

So Apple enters 2026 with no working AI solution yet and no real plans to monetize AI other than selling more iPhones. The exciting product news going into Fiscal Q1 '26 earnings on Thursday, January 29th, is the new AirTag product that sells for only $29.

Apple will apparently use Google Gemini to build the Apple Foundation Model to be utilized by Siri. Since Apple has already launched Apple Intelligence and has made this part of the basic iPhone software package for Siri, the tech giant doesn't appear to have a way to directly monetize AI.

The company plans to release the more personalized Siri voice assistant at the end of February. The recent stock dip from an all-time high of $288 is likely due to the final realization Apple doesn't have a method to monetize AI, outside of search via Google.

Big FQ1

With the holidays over, the focus will shift to the FQ1 numbers released on Thursday after the market closes. The consensus analyst estimates are as follows:

  • EPS of $2.67, up 11.3% YoY.
  • Revenue of $138.5 billion, up 11.4% YoY.

The big focus of the earnings call will be whether Apple has any ability to sustain growth in the double-digit range going forward. The market hasn't really explained the iPhone 17 sales boost with the new iPhone Air failing to drive growth and the hot iPhone 17 Pro version lacking any real AI in Apple Intelligence.

Most notably, IDC has predicted iPhone sales in 2025 of 247.4 million units, highest level on record. The amount tops the record of 236 million units back in 2021, suggesting Apple benefitted more from an upgrade cycle after 4 years than any real strong performance from a hot new AI product.

In fact, IDC appears to suggest iOS smartphone sales will dip next year due to the push out of the next iPhone base model from 2026 to early 2027 along with memory supply shortages. The forecast is for a sharp decline of 4.2% units followed by a 5.4% rebound in 2027, leading to the only revenue growth potential from iPhones the next 2 years due to higher ASPs.

For this reason, the expectation is for Apple to watch sales dip back to the mid-single digits going forward, similar to consensus analyst forecasts. The company is forecast to grow FY27 revenues by only 6.7%, and the EPS target is forecast to grow 10.6% to $9.14.

The whole world is currently in an investment cycle spending on AI, yet Apple has just focused on leveraging the business up via squeezing more profits out of the dated iPhone.

Apple faces a much bigger threat, as Meta Platforms (META) has a viable smart glasses product. Meta is already working with the Ray-Ban owner to boost smart glasses production to 20 to 30 million units this year in the first real product that is a potential future replacement for both the iPhone and the Apple Watch.

Even with the recent pullback, the stock trades at 27x FY27 EPS targets. As well, Apple requires upwards of $100 billion in aggressive annual share buybacks in order to achieve the EPS boost above the revenue growth. With a $3.65 trillion market cap, the $110 billion buyback from FY25 provides a nearly 3% boost to EPS.

Apple is busy spending every dollar of free cash flow from operations (only produced $111B in CFO in FY25) to boost short-term earnings growth, while Anthropic has already built an AI business with $9 billion in ARR. The generative AI company is looking for a fundraising of $350 billion, highlighting the opportunity Apple has missed out on.

The tech giant would be better suited to invest in AI and/or smart glasses technology than trying to maximize profits. As discussed in prior research, Apple should trade around 15x forward EPS targets with the forecast for a return to 5% to 6% sales growth, placing the stock at a valuation of ~$137.

While Apple recently hit all-time highs, the stock has actually underperformed the market over the last 5 years. This underperformance is likely to persist with no real AI monetization and a stock trading at a premium valuation of nearly 3x the growth rate while spending virtually all free cash flow on share buybacks at an inflated stock price.

Apple has a large installed user base, so the company could still figure out how to monetize the user base with AI. The Services division is still growing at a 15% annual clip, so the tech giant still has a path to growth with or without an AI boost.

Also, Apple has AR/VR technology and plans for smart glasses. The company could come through with new technology to upset the current first mover advantage of Meta with expectations for Apple Glasses in 2026, though likely to slip to 2027.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Apple is set to report a solid FQ1. The big question is whether the rebounding growth rate is sustainable, and all signs suggest this isn't the case.

Investors should've used the recent run to all-time highs to cash out since the current price is still very expensive for a company not even participating in the growth area of the next decade.

data.title: VFQY And VFMF: Quality Vs. Multifactor Exposure
data.description:
In this article, I look at two U.S. factor-based ETFs from Vanguard. The Vanguard U.S. Quality Factor ETF Shares (VFQY) and the Vanguard U.S. Multifactor ETF Shares (VFMF). My objective here is to determine the benefits provided by the quality screening process (in terms of VFQY) and compare it to the broader multifactor approach (in terms of VFMF).

My investment thesis is the following: Based on current sector tilts facing mixed headwinds, and both funds having failed to protect against the downside during multiple recent major market selloffs, I rate VFQY and VFMF a ‘Hold’.

While valuation discounts exist for both funds, historical underperformance compared to the benchmark and lack of any clear catalysts leave me remaining cautious.

Overview of the Funds

Both VFQY and VFMF have an inception dating back to February 2018, providing approximately 8 years of performance and fund management to consider. According to the funds’ website, the objective of these funds is the following:

“[The fund(s) seek long-term capital appreciation by investing in] a diverse mix of stocks representing many different market capitalizations (large, mid, and small), market sectors, and industry groups.” – Prospectuses for VFQY and VFMF

Furthermore, having been around since 1975, Vanguard is one of the top investment management firms in the world.

The Investment Screening Processes

In terms of the investment screening process, VFQY uses a rules-based quantitative model to evaluate stocks. This process begins with a broad universe of US equities, which is then reduced based on numerical rankings considering fundamental factors.

Specifically, the fund aims to target firms with strong underlying fundamentals, meaning strong profitability and healthy balance sheets. The quality factor (as outlined in the fund’s prospectus) is determined by operating profitability, gross profitability, change in net operating assets, intangibles, and equity issuance. Firms with excessive levels of debt, volatile earnings, and weak/deteriorating fundamentals are penalized in the process. The Russell 3000 Index, as represented by the Vanguard Russell 3000 Index Fund ETF Shares (VTHR), is used as the underlying benchmark, and the fund is reconstituted annually.

For VFMF, the process again begins with a broad universe of US stocks. This universe is reduced based on factors such as value, momentum, quality, and price stability. A weighting process is used to allocate across several factors, meaning that firms must score well in multiple factors to meet the selection criteria. Similar to VFQY, VFMF invests in at least 80% US equities, utilizes the Russell 3000 as the underlying benchmark, and is reconstituted annually.

Where VFQY specifically focuses on quality, VFMF considers numerous factors as a way to smooth returns over time.

Holdings & Performance

Top Holdings

The two funds have very little overlap in terms of top ten holdings. Top holdings for VFQY include names such as Lam Research Corporation (LRCX), KLA Corporation (KLAC), Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK), Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD), and Walmart Inc. (WMT), among other popular names. In total, top ten holdings account for just 15.95% of total holdings, meaning that the fund is not overly concentrated in top positions.

For VFMF, top ten holdings include names such as Newmont Corporation (NEM), Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), Altria Group, Inc. (MO), Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD), and AbbVie Inc. (ABBV), among others. Top ten holdings account for 9.18%. With even less concentration towards the top holdings than VFQY, the fund is likewise broadly diversified across positions.

Sector Allocation

In terms of sector allocation, VFQY is much more concentrated in technology, as compared to VFMF. Technology accounts for 21.64% of holdings, with financials, industrials, consumer cyclical, consumer defensive, and health care all making up a sizable portion of the fund.

VFMF’s exposure to technology is reduced (when compared to VFQY). Top sectors include financials (27.00%), healthcare (14.02%), technology (13.89%), and consumer cyclical (12.76%). Exposure to remaining sectors remains below 10%.

For VFQY, technology remains a key driver of returns. With the AI space continuing to grow, ongoing investment into AI generally supports a long-term positive outlook for higher-quality tech companies. These firms tend to be better capitalized and more profitable. However, near-term risks include exuberant valuations, rising CapEx, and potential normalization of earnings. For these reasons, while VFQY’s quality screening process tends to favor more mature tech companies, appreciation to the upside could remain capped.

As for VFMF, the fund is largely allocated towards financials. This leads to an increase in sensitivity to macroeconomic uncertainty. While I believe banks and insurers will likely continue to benefit from stable earnings and returns, forward-looking performance remains connected to interest rate moves, credit conditions, and economic growth. Therefore, negative economic conditions could weigh negatively on the sector.

As for both funds, consumer cyclicals remain a key part of total holdings. While consumer spending remains a strong driver of growth, a slowdown in consumer activity (which is common in late-cycle environments similar to what we are in now) could be a drag on the sector, creating negative headwinds for both funds.

In total, forward-looking sector exposure for VFQY and VFMF suggests limited catalysts in terms of outperformance. With structural tilts to tech and financials, investors should continue to monitor ongoing sector performance, as both funds remain sensitive to macroeconomic uncertainty.

While the current sector allocations of VFQY and VFMF provide a snapshot of current portfolio exposure, it is important to keep in mind that forward returns will depend on how key sectors evolve amid shifting macroeconomic conditions and how effectively each fund’s factor-based methodology is able to adapt to those changes. This is further supported by the low-to-moderate turnover ratios for both funds. Based on the most recent semi-annual report for VFQY, the fund maintains 413 portfolio positions with a 20% turnover ratio. Likewise, VFMF maintains 561 portfolio positions with a 31% turnover ratio. While allocations will change over time, it tends to be a more gradual change.

Recent Performance

VFQY is up 7.62%, and VFMF is up 14.93% on a YoY basis. While VFMF has outperformed the Russell 3000 Index and S&P 500 (SPY), VFQY has underperformed both the index and SPY.

Looking out further, both funds have underperformed the index since their inception. Since February 2018, VFQY has returned 103.47%, and VFMF has returned 102.38%. In comparison, the Russell 3000 has returned 247.95% while the S&P 500 has returned 256.31%.

While recent returns have more closely tracked the broader market, long-term, both funds have significantly underperformed.

Valuation

When compared to VFMF, VFQY appears to be more on the expensive side. Price/earnings, price/book, price/sales, and price/cash flow are all elevated when compared to the multifactor fund.

However, using the Russell 3000 as our benchmark, we can see that both VFQY and VFMF have favorable valuation metrics when compared to the index. Even though the funds have historically underperformed the benchmark based on price returns, valuation metrics appear favorable.

VFQY

(Source: Fidelity)

Additional Factors

As for additional factors, both VFQY and VFMF maintain strong Seeking Alpha Momentum and Expense Grades, while having weak to average Dividend grades.

VFQY has a Momentum grade of B-, with six-month, three-year, and five-year total returns all exceeding the median of all other ETFs. Likewise, with an expense ratio of 0.13%, the fund receives a Seeking Alpha Expense grade of A.

VFMF has a Momentum grade of A-. Similar to VFQY, the fund also outperforms the median of all other ETFs in terms of total returns. Also, true to Vanguard form, the fund offers a low expense ratio of 0.18% (receiving a Seeking Alpha grade of A-)

Where both funds struggle is in dividend payments. With VFQY’s dividend yield of 1.13% and VFMF’s dividend yield of 1.47%, both funds offer yields lower than that of the median of all other ETFs (2.73%).

With muted overall returns compared to the broad market and benchmark, and similarly underwhelming dividend yields, I believe income-oriented investors will find better dividend-paying opportunities elsewhere.

ETF

Expense Ratio

VFQY

0.18%

0.07%

SPY

0.09%

(Source: Seeking Alpha)

Quality Factor vs. Multifactor

Since the inception of both funds in February 2018, the market has seen three major selloffs resulting in a >20% decline. During these periods, VFQY and VFMF did not fare any better than the broad market, despite their quality and multifactor screening processes.

Looking at the Covid-19 crash of 2020, the inflation induced market decline of 2022, and the early 2025 tariff scare, markets declined by roughly -34%, -27%, and -21%, respectively.

During these periods, VFQY saw peak-to-trough declines of approximately -37%, -36%, and -20%. This implies that, despite the screening process for high-quality stocks, in most cases, one would have been better off just investing in the broad market.

Likewise, during these three selloff periods, VFMF saw returns of approximately -41%, -25%, and -20%. Again, suggesting that despite the rigorous multifactor screening process, one would have been better off just investing in the broad market. This is further reinforced by the fact that both the benchmark Russell 3000 and S&P 500 saw better returns following these periods when compared to VFQY and VFMF.

Risks

Risks include concentration in the technology and financial sectors, as well as geographical risk.

With the funds invested in US equities, returns are entirely dependent on US stock market performance. With tariff uncertainty continuing into 2026, as well as geopolitical and economic tensions, investors should consider VFQY and VFMF in the context of their existing portfolio allocation.

Likewise, VFQY maintains 21.64% of holdings in technology, whereas VFMF maintains 27% in financials. Investors should factor this into their investment decision, especially if they already have significant exposure to the US technology and/or financial sectors.

Additionally, given the low-to-moderate portfolio turnover, both funds could be slow to react to major economic/market changes. As noted, changes in sector leadership may not be reflected in real time, resulting in holdings that do not adequately align with existing market conditions.

Conclusion

Since inception, both VFQY and VFMF have underperformed the benchmark Russell 3000 Index and S&P 500. While valuation metrics appear favorable (compared to the benchmark), based on current sector tilts facing mixed headwinds and both funds having failed to protect against the downside during multiple recent major market selloffs, I rate VFQY and VFMF a ‘Hold’. Investors should continue to monitor structural tilts to tech and financials, as well as ongoing sector performance, as both funds remain sensitive to evolving macroeconomic conditions.

data.title: BIT: Unsupported Distribution Indicates Only Downside For This CEF
data.description:
Thesis

We last covered the BlackRock Multi-Sector Income Trust (BIT), mid-2025 with a 'Hold' rating. The CEF has posted a small negative total return since, surprisingly, on the downside in an otherwise benign credit environment. In today's article, we are going to revisit the CEF and its composition, the latest developments for the fund, and outline our view given the current macro.

Rights offering

The CEF had a rights offering in September 2025 that resulted in more cash being deployed:

New York, September 9, 2025 - BlackRock Multi-Sector Income Trust (NYSE: BIT) (the "Fund") today announced the successful completion of its transferable rights offer (the “Offer”) which expired on September 9, 2025 (the “Expiration Date”). The Offer entitled rights holders to subscribe for up to an aggregate of 14,265,677 shares of the Fund’s common shares of beneficial interest, par value of $0.001 per share (each, a “Common Share”). The final subscription price of $13.01 per Common Share was determined based upon the formula equal to 90% of the net asset value as of 9/9/2025.

We saw weakness in the shares around the rights offering, which is normal:

Please note the -10% drop in the common shares once the rights offering was announced. A rights offering is ultimately a capital raise, and they are very successful only when the fund ends up using that cash to buy assets on the cheap. Let us see next what the current balance sheet looks like and see how the cash was used.

Current composition

The fund looks to have deployed the entire cash amount, still running a large leverage ratio:

The above table presents the holdings in the BlackRock fashion, which plugs the leverage as a negative balance in the 'Cash/Derivatives' line. Basically, the fund reports all its holdings as per the balances, including borrowed cash, and the borrowed amount is a negative amount.

We can see the CEF being overweight high yield at 50% of the portfolio at a time when high yield is extremely extended (in our view), followed by securitized products at 22% and Agency mortgages at 16%.

A more conservative stance for the CEF would have been to lower its leverage ratio with the cash that was raised. It is not the case.

The portfolio is fairly credit risky as per the current composition:

Sub-investment grade names make up most of the portfolio, currently representing over 70% of the fund holdings. Please also note the 'CCC' bucket is very large at 15% of the portfolio. We usually like to see CCC buckets at 10% or less. In a severe recession, these credits will experience a high rate of default and possibly very low recoveries.

Let us now look at other portfolio analytics as presented by the manager:

The fund has an average duration of 3.9 years, smack in the middle of what high-yield funds do. The portfolio yield to maturity is a low 7.15% figure, highlighting the tight markets, while the distribution is a high 11.27%, which we will see is unsupported. Please note the high leverage ratio presented by the fund at 29%, with the cash raised via the rights offering fully invested.

We do not like this move since it looks like the rights offering was just an opportunity for the CEF to move its AUM up so that it can clip fees, rather than a strategic move to position the portfolio for a tight credit environment. Let us remember how this fund does in a risk-off environment:

The CEF had a large -11% drawdown during the April 2025 market risk-off event, with a regular recession possibly bringing -20% or more in terms of a drawdown.

So the CEF used the rights offering to just pad its depleted AUM rather than deleverage. We do not like this move.

Unsupported distribution

For BIT, when looking at the portfolio analytics, it is easy to see the distribution is not supported since the math does not add up. But let us look at Section 19a as well for that:

We can see the CEF with a large 31% utilization of return of capital ('ROC'), which is not surprising. Again, we do not like this, and we usually draw a line at 20% to 25% ROC usage.

Given the tight credit conditions, do not expect the CEF to cover its dividend better. Again, a negative for the fund. We would have much more liked a dividend cut to see less ROC utilization and a lower leverage ratio that would position the CEF more conservatively.

Performance has been poor

The CEF has had a very disappointing performance in the past year:

The performance in the past year for the CEF has been nothing short of dreadful. The name is negative on a total return basis, all while a simple ETF like the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) is up roughly 8% with lower volatility metrics. Well-run CEFs like the PIMCO Income Strategy Fund II (PFN) are up 13% in the same time period.

Investors buy active credit CEFs for total returns, not for managers to pad AUM in order to have high fees. BIT has not delivered, and moreover, its latest corporate actions are not positioning the CEF for success in our view.

Spreads are tight

When we look at historic U.S. spreads, we get a picture of very tight levels:

The ICE BofA US High Yield Index OAS is at 10-year lows currently, with spreads at only 2.64%. Can they stay low? Yes. Will any deviation from a perfect economic path make spreads blow up? Absolutely. We got a flavor of that in April 2025 when spreads moved above 4% and BIT got a drawdown in excess of -10%. In our view, the next move for spreads will be up following some sort of black swan event in 2026 or a plain recession.

Conclusion

BIT is a fixed income CEF. The name takes a multi sector approach, but is overweight U.S. high yield. The fund had a rights offering in September 2025, but used the cash to buy more assets rather than deleverage. The CEF has a high 29% gearing ratio in an environment where credit spreads are very tight.

With an unsupported distribution and a portfolio with significant credit risk, we only see downside in 2026 for this fund. We do not usually give 'Strong Sell' ratings, but BIT does deserve one at this juncture. To note, we used to own this fund in 2021/2022 when its interest rate hedging helped the fund outperform its peers; hence, we are not biased in terms of rating this CEF.

data.title: Consumer Confidence Plummets To Lowest Level Since 2014
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index plummeted to its lowest level since 2014 in January, dropping 9.7 points to 84.5. This is the steepest drop in over four years and was lower than the forecast of 90.6. The latest reading puts the index in the 28th percentile of the data series.

The Present Situation Index, which is based on consumers' assessment of current business and labor market conditions, fell 9.9 points to 113.7. Meanwhile, the Expectations Index, which is based on consumers' short-term outlook for income, business, and labor market conditions, dropped 9.5 points to 65.1. Note that a level of 80 or below for the Expectations Index historically signals a recession within the next year, and the index has been below 80 since February 2025.

“Confidence collapsed in January, as consumer concerns about both the present situation and expectations for the future deepened,” said Dana M Peterson, Chief Economist, The Conference Board. “All five components of the Index deteriorated, driving the overall Index to its lowest level since May 2014 (82.2)—surpassing its COVID-19 pandemic depths.”

Peterson added: “Consumers’ write-in responses on factors affecting the economy continued to skew towards pessimism. References to prices and inflation, oil and gas prices, and food and grocery prices remained elevated. Mentions of tariffs and trade, politics, and the labor market also rose in January, and references to health/insurance and war edged higher.”

Read more

Background on the Consumer Confidence Index

The Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index measures the consumers' attitudes and confidence in the economy, business conditions, and labor market, with higher readings indicating higher optimism. The general assumption is that when consumers are more optimistic, they will spend more and stimulate economic growth.

However, if consumers are pessimistic, then spending will decline, and the economy may slow down. The index is based on a 5 question survey, with 2 questions related to present conditions and 3 questions related to future expectations. The survey began in 1967 and was conducted every two months but changed to monthly reporting in 1977, which is where our data begins.

The next two charts are attempts to evaluate the historical context for this index as a coincident indicator of the economy. In this chart, I have highlighted recessions and the level the index was at at the start of each recession. The average of the consumer confidence index at the start of recessions is 101.9, a level we have been below since February 2025.

In this next chart, I have included an overlay with the GDP. It is easy to see the dips in consumer confidence when GDP is negative.

The next chart includes regression lines through the consumer confidence index and through the GDP series. Interestingly, the GDP regression has a slightly negative slope while consumer confidence has been increasing over the same time frame (since the late 1970s). It's clear that consumer confidence begins falling shortly before official recession calls.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, let's take a look at the correlation between consumer confidence and small business sentiment, the latter by way of the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index.

The consumer measure is the more volatile of the two, so it is plotted on a separate axis to give a better comparison of the two series from the common baseline of 100.

As the chart illustrates, the two have tracked one another fairly closely since the onset of the financial crisis. The two have diverged at brief periods and been highly correlated at others.

ETFs associated with sentiment include the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Pure Storage: Earnings Growth Does Not Support Its Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

It’s been well over 2 years since my first article on Pure Storage (PSTG), so it was time for a quick update to see how the company has performed and what is still in store in terms of growth in earnings and the operational pivot towards a high-margin business model.

The pivot has been happening since at least my first article, and I mentioned that the AI tailwinds will help it, but I expected much better performance in profitability, which, for this reason, I am maintaining a hold rating as I do not find this growth particularly appealing.

There are better AI plays out there with much more reasonable valuations.

Briefly on Performance

Looking at the company’s top-line performance, we can see steady growth over the last 2 years. It’s not as impressive as many other players that have some sort of involvement in the AI space, but there is a reason why this is the case for PSTG. Nevertheless, the company’s average quarterly growth for the last 2 years is around 12%-13%, which was helped by healthy growth in subs and AI tailwinds.

Let’s also take a look at the company’s profitability and efficiency over the last 2 years. I am quite surprised to see that the margins are not performing the way I thought they would. Since the company is very keen on pivoting towards a subscription-based model, I would have imagined that margins across the board would begin to increase. However, that is not what we are seeing here. At least for now. The product category still dominates in terms of total revenue contributions, although subscriptions are gaining momentum, and since products are much more costly, especially because of investments in R&D and sales ramp for AI, this will continue for a bit longer, as the product mix is focused on volume pricing to gain market share.

Just like the margins across the board aren’t looking the best, so do the other key metrics I like to look at afterwards, which are the ROA, ROE, and ROTC. Here we can see ROA is just pushing 3%, while ROE is almost passable at 9%. I would like to see at least 10% for ROE and 5% for ROA. Additionally, I am not very satisfied with the company’s ROTC. Here, I would like to see at least 10%. At around 2.6% TTM ROTC, that is not appealing, and definitely not worth the premium the company seems to be demanding with regard to its current PE ratio of 194x and a FW PE ratio of over 37x. No moat or pricing power here.

Now, what about the company’s financial position? PSTG finished Q3 ’26 with around $853m in cash and equivalents as well as $679m in marketable securities. The only debt I see on the balance sheet is some operating leases, which is a great position to be in. All the capital resources it makes can be used to further the growth of the company to keep relevant, grow market share, and reward shareholders. Free cash flow for the quarter was around $414m, up from $374.5m at the same time last quarter. The company is more profitable and has a decent amount of capital to continue to work on its profitability without any worries of running out of cash anytime soon. That alone can persuade people to invest in the company.

Overall, I think the company has been struggling a bit operationally. Its share price has gone up decently since my first article back in November of 2023, but I don’t see why. The company’s profitability waned, with only positives coming right now from the AI tailwinds on its top-line growth and the overall AI growth story, which, if compared to other AI plays, PSTG is vastly underperforming.

Comments on the Outlook

Pivot to subscriptions model can’t come soon enough

All of this growth in the topline is welcome; however, when products continue to contribute more than half of the company’s total revenues, it is hard to see any decent scale potential. Products naturally come with lower margins and demand a lot more investments to keep the market share, which means lower pricing to sell through more product. The company’s GAAP margins are already very close to zero, and with further ramp in additional investment for hyperscaler, I can see these margins continue to deteriorate further. So, further gross margin pressures are in the cards; however, that is something that is acceptable in the short term. The pain is for long-term gain, which means that with future investments into hyperscaler business, the software in this space tends to command higher margins overall, which should translate into higher profitability in the long run.

The company expects to see a massive increase in its operating profit, of around 47% y/y in adjusted terms, mind you, which gives us a non-GAAP operating margin of around 17% for the FY26. This is slightly below FY25’s 17.7%, so I am wondering when the company is going to see a decent profitability increase. Everything so far points towards sometime next year, when the company will be able to capture a lot more of the high-margin hyperscaler business, which I believe will be subscriptions business.

Hyperscalers are spending an exorbitant amount of capital on this AI race, and there is no stopping anytime soon. I believe that companies like Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT) will opt for the subscription-based model if they were to contract with PSTG because the technology is progressing so quickly right now, leasing PSTG’s product instead of owning it will make more sense since they can then replace the old technology with the newer offering after a couple of years, avoiding obsolete product, and rinse and repeat every few years.

This also means that PSTG is not going to see a massive explosion in its top line because of the subscription-based model, which allows for predictability and certainty of cash flows, unlike the more cyclical companies that depend on hyperscalers to buy up their product.

Analysts are expecting a decent growth in earnings (non-GAAP) over the next half a decade; however, the number of analysts drops off significantly after FY28, but even then, the company’s forward PE ratio is still at over 26x at these prices, which I view as still quite expensive given its profitability.

Valuation

Let’s take a look at a quick valuation model to see what the company is worth to me at the current analysts’ projections and some less-than-conservative additional assumptions made by me. Below are the assumptions I used.

Analysts expect a decent mid-teens growth in sales over the long term, so I went with that too. For operating margins, clearly everyone is focused on adjusted profits, and these are quite different from its GAAP performance, so I went with a decent operating margin improvement. Hyperscalers and further pivot into a subscription-based model will help it achieve higher profitability overall. The rest of the model I kept more or less at the company’s historical averages to keep it simple. I believe operating margins are the ones to drive the company’s value anyway. I am also using the company’s WACC of around 9.3% as my discount rate, which I think is sufficient in this case. Below are the EPS projections we get, which are more or less in line with analysts’ projections.

With that said, PSTG’s intrinsic value is around $40 right now, which means there is just not enough growth in earnings for me to add the company to my already heavily-weighted AI portfolio.

Closing Comments

I could see the future share price continue its climb due to the AI tailwinds and the like, but I don’t think the growth is going to be as explosive as other AI names that have been growing their earnings at a much faster pace and are currently trading at low-teens PE ratios. PSTG has potential, but so far, I am not a fan of the company’s forecasted earnings growth.

I will revisit in a couple of quarters to see how this has progressed and whether I need some adjustments in my model to account for higher earnings potential.

data.title: Ford Dominates Emerging Market For Compact Pickups, A Positive For Shell-Shocked Investors
data.description:
Ford Motor Co. (F) absorbed a blow to its balance sheet on Dec. 15 with the massive restructuring charge of $19.5 billion, which coincided with the decision to scrap its slow-selling F-150 Lightning battery-electric pickup and substantially de-emphasize battery electric vehicles (BEVs) for the near future.

Ford may be a bit wobbly from its BEV misadventures, but the No. 2 U.S. automaker after General Motors Co. (GM) still has fight left. The automaker has a strategy to repurpose its battery assets. More about that shortly. Ford also hasn’t lost its grasp of the U.S. vehicle market or what makes it tick, evidenced by a niche it's pioneering and now dominates: Compact pickup trucks.

Maverick was introduced in the fall of 2021, making it the smallest in a pickup truck lineup by Ford’s F150 full-size (and larger Super Duty versions), followed by the midsize Ranger pickup. The introduction was a gamble that some car buyers would choose the usability and fuel efficiency of small pickups while disfavoring the rougher ride and handling characteristics similar to larger models.

As of the end of 2025, the bet appears to have paid off: Maverick sales have more than doubled from its first year of production to finish the latest annual year with sales of 155,000 units. Journalists awarded the model “Truck of the Year” at the Detroit Auto Show earlier this month.

Meanwhile, Maverick now outsells Ranger and has only one other competitor in its class, Hyundai Santa Cruz, which sold about 25,500 in 2025.

More Car Than Truck

One key to Maverick’s success is that it’s actually, in one important sense, a four-passenger car with a bed attached, rather than a true pickup. The larger F150 and Ranger are built with body-on-frame architectures, giving them more hauling and towing capability; the Maverick and other cars feature unibody construction, which delivers better fuel efficiency and a smoother ride for passengers than pickups.

Maverick’s underpinning, designated C2 by Ford, was used for the Bronco Sport and Escape crossover. Among the engine options is a gas-electric hybrid. The retail price for the entry-level version is just under $30,000—making it relatively affordable and economical to operate as daily transportation.

A question that only Ford accounting executives could answer is the model’s absolute and relative profitability and its impact on Ranger, whose sales have lagged behind those of Maverick. Ford research shows more than half of Maverick buyers came from small SUVs and other cars. Generally speaking, the larger and more expensive the vehicle model, the more profit derived from it.

The biggest profit contributor by far remains Ford’s big F Series pickup models, which sold more than 828,000 copies in 2025. The average retail selling price for F Series trucks was about $66,000. Institutional analysts have typically estimated $10,000 to $13,000 of average operating profit per F Series truck, making it by far the most important single product that Ford produces.

Maverick, by contrast, represents only a niche in the market and accounts for a small part of Ford’s financial output—yet it remains an important hedge in the event that gasoline prices skyrocket or that segment gains broad popularity.

When Ford posts its fourth quarter 2025 financial results on Feb. 10, Maverick will occupy little, if any, of the investing public’s immediate interest. Shareholders likely will want to know the guidance for the rest of 2026. As the company pivots away from its previous electrification strategy, the major unknowns will be how tariffs are likely to impact profit, the strength of consumer demand for Ford’s bread-and-butter trucks, and, finally, whether the new electrification strategy toward affordable EVs by 2030 is viable.

Lower Cost Better

Ford has said, under its revised electrification strategy, it will “concentrate its North American electric vehicle development on its new, low-cost, flexible Universal EV Platform. This next-generation architecture is engineered to underpin a high-volume family of smaller, highly efficient, and affordable electric vehicles designed to be accessible to millions of customers.”

The cancelled F-150 Lightning BEV will be reborn as an extended range electric vehicle (EREV), a propulsion configuration that includes a gasoline engine continuously charging a battery that powers electric motors.

“Our next-generation Lightning EREV is every bit as revolutionary,” said Doug Field, Ford’s chief EV, digital, and design officer. “It keeps everything customers love—100% electric power delivery and sub-5-second acceleration—and adds an estimated 700-plus mile range and tows like a locomotive. It will be an incredibly versatile tool delivered in a capital-efficient way.”

Forget the Plugs

Most importantly, EREVs don’t need charging, so they don’t come with questions about range and charging that hurt the previous generation’s BEV.

Another concern for investors is whether Ford can successfully repurpose its written-off battery assets toward a new and profitable business, one that hasn’t received much publicity. On Dec. 15, Ford announced plans to convert two battery plants—one in Kentucky, the other in Michigan—for the production of battery energy storage systems - BESS - for data centers and the electric grid, as well as smaller systems for residential application.

A word of caution: The commercial battery storage business is highly competitive, with players such as Tesla (TSLA) that have—unlike Ford—been at it for a while.

A second word of caution: Chinese automakers are a step closer to entering the U.S. Geely disclosed at CES in January its intention to decide about U.S. entry in the next 24 to 36 months; Trump has riffed on being OK with a Chinese plant in the U.S.; and Canada is making accommodations for the import of 49,000 low-cost Chinese BEVs.

Since April, Ford shares have gained 48% in value, outperforming the S&P 500—possibly an implicit sign of approval from investors that the automaker is returning to its strengths and making the hard decisions, as reflected by the big write-off. For the past two years, Ford has declared a special dividend in addition to its regular quarterly dividend in February—no one will be surprised if a special dividend is omitted or reduced this year.

Ford is still pretty cheap at 11.88 times forward earnings. Nevertheless, hold Ford shares until guidance is established in February and possibly until Q1 profitability can be measured.

data.title: 'Reversion To The Mean' Will Eventually Slam The Market
data.description:
It is hard to describe the current market environment. On one hand, equities are trading near valuation levels last seen during the end of the Internet Boom, just over a quarter century ago. By some measures, stocks are in uncharted territory. On the other hand, nothing seems to even dent the momentum in equities for more than a day or so. Last Tuesday's over two percent blip down for the NASDAQ and S&P 500 already is long forgotten by investors, it seems.

One adjective that comes to mind describing the U.S. market is resilient. Another good word to capture the investment community's view on equities is complacency. One key reason for this might be over 60% of trading volume is driven by passive investing, which I highlighted in this recent article.

At this point, it is anybody's guess what will finally knock the wind out of this more than three-year rally in equities. Maybe the AI bubble will pop. Maybe the trigger will be growing problems in the credit markets, as I discussed in this recent piece.

Perhaps the markets will finally be done in by these four words: 'reversion to the mean.' The rally in the market has been primarily driven by the Magnificent Seven since ChatGPT debuted in November 2022, and enthusiasm around the AI Revolution was birthed. The top ten largest stocks in the market now account for roughly 40% of the entire market. A concentration not seen since the last gasps of the Internet Boom.

It is easy to see why the Mag7 has delivered most of the equity gains over the past few years. This is where almost all the profit growth across the S&P 500 has come from. This can be seen in the chart above. The Mag7 has delivered consistent over 20% annual earnings growth since 2023. Profit growth from the 'S&P 493' has been a tepid three percent over that time.

In addition, earnings growth has also benefited in recent quarters from aggressive cost-cutting (one key reason job growth faltered in 2025) and huge stock buybacks. Companies had announced over $1 trillion worth of stock buybacks by August of last year, an all-time record.

As the chart above shows, the operating margins for the S&P 500 have moved up significantly in recent years. This increase has entirely been driven by the technology sector. However, this is largely due to the financing circle throughout the AI ecosystem. Basically, large tech giants are selling and contracting for goods and services from each other.

The 'poster boy' for this circular activity is OpenAI. The creator of ChatGPT has over $1.4 trillion in commitments for future computing power. Roughly 100 times its revenues in FY2025, and the company is bleeding cash. What possibly could go wrong in this story?

There are also increasing questions around whether AI-related revenues, outside those for the chip makers, will emerge to justify the hundreds of billions of dollars being invested annually on building out AI infrastructure. McKinsey & Co. recently projected that over $6.5 trillion globally will be spent cumulatively by 2030 on AI. Roughly half of that will be spent on CPUs from the likes of NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA) and other components that are put into massive AI data centers.

However, AI-related annual revenues are only expected to grow to $300 billion by 2029. While that is an impressive growth trajectory, it doesn't even begin to justify the projected spending on the AI ecosystem.

The divergence between AI-related revenues and costs could eventually rear its ugly head for many of the firms driving this rally. Morgan Stanley recently slashed its price target on Oracle (ORCL) by a whopping third to $213 a share. The investment bank did so as it believes Oracle's 'rapid buildout of artificial intelligence infrastructure will likely push earnings per share results to fall below expected targets and increase capital expenditures.' Of note, Oracle inked a huge $300 billion deal with OpenAI last summer. The company is adding tens of billions of debt to build out this capacity.

In addition, the increase in corporate profit margins over the past few decades has increasingly come at the cost of employee compensation. This accelerated markedly at the start of the century thanks primarily to China joining the WTO, migration patterns, and productivity improvements via the birth of the internet.

This has resulted in an increasingly bifurcated economy, which many have dubbed the K-shaped economy. The top 10% of households in the U.S. now account for roughly half of all consumer spending, up from the low 30s in the early 1990s. This trend, in my opinion, is one of the key factors in the increasing political division in the U.S.

I also believe this is one of the key reasons U.S. consumer sentiment readings are dismal despite four percent headline GDP growth and a 4.4% unemployment rate.

The markets are clearly in overvalued territory judged from a historical perspective. The Shiller CAPE ratio, which adjusts for inflation and smooths cycles over a decade, stands near 41. Forward P/E estimates for 2026 earnings are over 23x.

Of note, every time the S&P 500 has reached a valuation of 23 times forward PE or higher, the resultant annual returns over the next decade have averaged zero percent within a relatively narrow range of -2% annually to a positive two percent appreciation.

Will it be different this time around? Analyst firms believe profit margins can go higher in 2026, despite already being at historically extreme levels. Color me skeptical on this front.

With the markets at these valuation levels, investors have the tide of history working against them over the longer run. Eventually the markets are going to be slammed to a reversion to the mean move as they have been in previous stock market bubbles.

A bubble is a bull market in which you don't have a position.”- Eddy Elfenbein

However, when that event occurs is anybody's guess. Equity markets were clearly overvalued at the conclusion of 1998. Which was two years after Alan Greenspan's famous 'irrational exuberance' remarks about stock market valuations. However, 1999 saw another surge in equity returns for investors. This did turn out to be a blow-off top in retrospect. The Internet Bust would commence in early 2000, driving the NASDAQ down some 80% before the markets bottomed in August of 2022.

Therefore, I continue to participate in the market despite trepidations around overall market valuations. Roughly three-quarters of my portfolio allocation is within covered call holdings around the few stocks I am finding in this market with reasonable valuations. The rest is largely in short-term treasuries and cash. That will likely continue to be my portfolio stance until equities hit their inevitable 'reversion to the mean' bear market at some point in the future.

data.title: Kimberly-Clark Just Proved Itself As Its Purchase Of Kenvue Nears (Upgrade)
data.description:
On the morning of January 27th, the management team at Kimberly-Clark Corporation (KMB) announced financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Even though revenue and adjusted earnings per share fell short of analysts’ expectations, GAAP earnings per share exceeded what was anticipated. On the whole, the company is showing some signs of struggling from a revenue standpoint, but these are modest. Profitability is improving, proving that the company is doing define job as it continues to focus on its strategic transformation initiatives.

What's more, the company's acquisition of Kenvue Inc. (KVUE) is progressing nicely, and that opens the door to meaningful upside if things go according to plan.

In general, I would say that the picture is currently more favorable than it is unfavorable. However, there are certain risk factors, primarily associated with its purchase of Kenvue. Despite this, however, the company is looking more appealing now than it was previously. In fact, relative to other comparable firms, the stock is attractively priced. And if we end up seeing synergies come from its merger with Kenvue, upside potential could be quite nice. Given this, I believe that upgrading the stock to a soft Buy from the Hold I had it rated previously is the appropriate choice right now.

A Mixed Bag With Promise

Before the market opened on January 27th, the management team at Kimberly-Clark announced financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. The quarter was most certainly a mixed bag if you ask me. Revenue as an example. It contracted ever so slightly from $4.10 billion to $4.08 billion, ultimately missing analysts estimates to the tune of $10 million.

This does require a bit of detail, though. Actual organic sales for the company increased by 2.1%. It was only because of the firm's decision to exit its private label diaper business in the U.S. that revenue contracted. The organic sales growth, according to the company, can be chalked up to rising volume and a change in product mix that, combined, added 3% to the top line. If only it weren't for the fact that it took a 1.1% hit associated with price investments, which I interpret as price cuts aimed at boosting sales, that revenue didn't rise further.

The bottom line for the company is a bit more complex. There's a lot going on here. This includes significant earnings from discontinued operations, net income and losses associated with non-controlling interests, and the share of net income of equity companies that the business is entitled to. What we do know is that, when we strip out the income from discontinued operations and incorporate everything else that's important, actual earnings per share came in at $1.14. That's up from the $1.03 per share reported the previous year and exceeded analysts’ estimates by $0.12. This meant a rise in net profits from $344 million to $380 million.

On an adjusted basis, we got a jump from $1.19 per share to $1.43 per share. Although this is a huge increase, translating to a rise in adjusted net income from $397 million to $477 million, it does mean that the company fell short of estimates by $0.32 on a per share basis.

Other profitability metrics certainly deserve our attention. For instance, operating cash flow for the company jumped from $817 million to $972 million. Removing changes in working capital, the increase was much smaller from $793 million to $796 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company increased from $656 million to $673 million. In the chart above, you can also see a financial results for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue contracted, and most profitability metrics for the company dropped also. But there were some bright spots here.

Although you can argue that the final quarter was a mixed bag for the company, I do think that it demonstrated definitively that management is achieving success when it comes to major initiatives. Back in 2024, the company embarked on a transformation initiative aimed at simplifying the business and cutting costs. Over the two years that it has been working on this, it has achieved remarkable success. This included an optimization of its supply network through a $2 billion investment that the company made in North America centered around advanced manufacturing. This involved a facility in Warren, Ohio, as well as a new distribution center at its Beech Island facility.

The firm invested in transforming its procurement operations from a digital standpoint. It implemented new technology as the standard for supply chain planning. It focused on automation initiatives when it came to its manufacturing and logistics facilities. And it also focused on the product side, such as by reducing 30 product platforms to 11 across nine of its manufacturing facilities. The firm is currently half of the way done through its targets for the five years that the project in question was focused on. So in all likelihood, we should expect further improvements moving forward.

Management also continues to invest in innovation. This has included launches of new offerings under its Huggies, Depend, Goodnites, and other brands. In international markets, it's also targeting expansion. China, for instance, has been a major source of focus for the company as it invests heavily in its social media platforms there. It also achieved significant growth in Indonesia thanks to its digital efforts.

These efforts should certainly bear fruit this year. Management expects, for instance, to achieve organic sales growth this year that's in line with or ahead of market growth. The weighted average market growth for 2025 was about 2%, so we should expect organic sales growth to be that or slightly higher.

You would think that, for a business like this, this would mean a small amount of improvement from a profitability standpoint. However, the company is targeting double-digit constant currency adjusted earnings per share growth from continuing operations for the year. And even though it is accelerating its capital expenditures for the year, with a target of $1.3 billion, it still expects adjusted free cash flow for 2026 of $2 billion.

Management has made other moves also. For instance, it is still in the process of selling off its International Family Care and Professional segment. Back in June of last year, the company announced that it will be selling a 51% ownership stake of that enterprise in exchange for $1.7 billion. It will keep the remaining 49% equity in that firm. If everything goes according to plan, this transaction will close sometime in the middle of this year. And the proceeds from it will be allocated toward the $6.73 billion of the cash component of its acquisition of Kenvue. We don't yet have a date for when the purchase of Kenvue should close. But it is almost certain to be in the second half of this year.

Because of all of the changes going on with the company, I don't think that analyzing it in a vacuum makes sense. Instead, I think that valuing it compared to what it will look like after its purchase of Kenvue is the right choice. This does involve factoring in changes in share count, net debt, and incorporating the aforementioned sale of IFP. But at the end of the day, when you add the figures together, the picture looks interesting to say the least.

In the chart above, you can see how Kimberly-Clark is valued on its own using results from 2024 and 2025. You can also see how Kenvue is valued, both as the company is being bought for (which incorporates projected results for 2025 less after tax interest expense associated with the new indebtedness that Kimberly-Clark is taking on to make the deal happen) and net of the $2.10 billion of synergies that management expects to capture from the transaction. The chart also shows what the combined company will look like from a valuation standpoint, both without synergies and with them. What we see here is a business that, on the whole, looks pretty much fairly valued. But if synergies do come to fruition, it is certainly in the undervalued range.

Relative to other similar firms, however, the stock is attractively priced. In the chart below, you can see how five other similar firms compared to it. On a standalone basis, only one of the five companies is cheaper than it is when it comes to the price to earnings and EV to EBITDA approaches. And on a price to operating cash flow basis, our prospect is the cheapest of the group. On a combined basis, without synergies, the only thing that changes is that it's tied as the cheapest when it comes to two of the three valuation metrics. And with synergies, the company suddenly becomes the cheapest across the board.

Takeaway

From what I can see, the overall picture for shareholders of Kimberly-Clark is positive. Yes, revenue dropped recently. But organic revenue is up. Profits and cash flows are improving thanks to the initiatives that management has embarked on. And while there is some risk associated with its purchase of Kenvue, even if synergies aren't realized, the company is still relatively appealing. This creates a favorable risk to reward situation that justifies, in my view, upgrading the stock from a Hold to a soft Buy.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Why Super Investors Like It At $17
data.description:
ET stock: Strong money kept adding at around $17

I last analyzed Energy Transfer LP stock (ET) on Dec. 6 with an article titled "$50 Oil Price Scenario: Enterprise Products Partners Better Positioned Than Energy Transfer." The article focused on the impact of oil prices and ended with a hold rating for ET. Since then, a few new developments have caught my attention and triggered this revisit. This article will focus on the top three on my list: The update to its Lake Charles LNG project, the new guidance on its 2026 growth capex, and also the disclosures from the professional fund managers.

Let me start backward with the third development as it is the most straightforward one. The next chart shows the latest statistics of ET share ownership among the so-called super-investors by DataRoma. These are professional fund managers, and I have benefited from these managers’ insights in the past. In my experience, they tend to be less emotional, more oriented toward the longer term, and also have a deeper understanding of the fundamentals of the business they invest in. If my experiences relate to you too, you can easily see why their decisions surrounding ET caught my attention. Firstly, these investors have disproportionate exposure to ET. As shown, ET is currently owned by five of them with a total ownership rank of 97. Several of the super investors have quite concentrated exposure as high as 7.0% and 3.3% (by Leon Cooperman and Samantha McLemore). Such exposures are quite large when adjusted for ET’s cap of around $62 B. As background, the market cap would put ET somewhere around the 180th largest company in the S&P 500 index (see the second chart below), vs. an ownership rank of 97 among these super investors.

Moreover, these investors’ latest decisions reflect a quite bullish sentiment toward ET. As seen, none of them reported a reduction in their position, and one of them reported a sizable increase of almost 20% recently. Last but not least, note that their holding price is around $17.20, quite close to the current market price. As to be elaborated on in the final section, insiders have also disclosed purchases around this price recently in November 2025. This is not too different from the stock’s current market price of $18 (a difference of only ~5%). As such, I feel it's timely for potential investors to examine their holdings and ponder why they like ET shares at these price levels. I will provide my 2 cents next.

ET stock: Lake Charles and capex update

In a nutshell, I consider ET at the current price levels a good combination of value, growth, and yield. The stock currently yields 7.4% and trades at reasonable valuation ratios (more on this in the next section). Looking ahead, I see excellent prospects for its dividend distribution to grow at healthy rates (say in the 3% to 5% range) supported by an expanding asset base with exceptional product and geographic diversity. This leads me to the following update on its Lake Charles project.

ET press release (Dec. 18, 2025) Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET) announced today that it is suspending development of the Lake Charles LNG project in order to focus on allocating capital to its significant backlog of natural gas pipeline infrastructure projects that Energy Transfer believes provides superior risk/return profiles. Energy Transfer management has determined that its continued development of the project is not warranted by Energy Transfer but remains open to discussions with third parties who may have an interest in developing the project.

The development could be considered a negative given how much time and resources the company has invested in this project over the years. ET’s decision to suspend at this late stage also reflects the new dynamics in the LNG space compared to the time the company started the project.

However, on the positive side, the suspension could free up new capital for ET to invest in other expansion projects with more promising returns. As a reflection, the company has also announced its growth capex guidance for 2026 in early January. According to new guidance, ET’s growth capital during 2026 is budgeted in the range of $5 billion-$5.5 billion with a focus on its natural gas network. To better contextualize these figures, the chart below shows the trend in capex for Energy Transfer in recent years. As seen, the TTM capex sits at a peak level of $5.652 billion in at least five years. This is also mirrored in terms of the capex to revenue ratio. At a current value of 0.071, the current ratio sits well above the historical average of 0.049 and is close to the peak level observed five years ago. The new guidance thus reflects continued focus on growth and is likely to fuel continued profit growth in my view, as detailed next.

ET stock: EPS growth outlook and return potential

Looking into ET’s EPS growth, I do see good potential due to the factors mentioned above. As a reflection, the current consensus EPS estimates for ET is shown in chart below, and they point to healthy growth in the years to come. To wit, ET’s EPS is projected to grow at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of ~9.4% in the next six fiscal years. I do see several growth catalysts ongoing to support such a growth curve. In particular, judging by its above 2026 capex guidance, I see the potential of multiple expansion projects. Notably, several of the expansion projects were related to data center agreements, which should enjoy a secular tailwind in my model given how power-hungry AI applications are.

With a healthy pace of EPS growth of almost 10%, ET’s current valuation is quite attractive. The PEG ratio (P/E growth ratio) is only about 1.4x with a FWD P/E ratio of 13.6x. The valuation ratio is even more attractive when adjusted for yield. The stock currently offers a dividend yield of 7.34%. Applying the following PEGY ratio promoted by Peter Lynch, the stock’s current PEGY ratio is only 0.81x (assuming a FWD P/E of 13.6x and a growth rate of 9.4% on average).

For dividend stocks, Lynch uses a revised version of the PEG ratio - the PEGY ratio, which is defined as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The idea behind the PEGY is very simple and effective (most effective ideas are simple). If a stock pays out a large part of its earnings as dividends, then investors do not need a high growth rate to enjoy healthy returns. And vice versa. And similar to the PEG ratio, his preference is a PEGY ratio of 1x or below.

Downside risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, the impact of oil prices is certainly a concern (which was the focus of my last article). As a downside to its many ongoing/planned expansion projects, some of these capex projects could have a prolonged timeframe and thus uncertain return prospects. A notable example on my list involves the Waha asset. With many other gas pipelines coming online in that basin area, I see ET’s Waha asset facing increased uncertainties both in terms of duration and spread. To further compound these uncertainties, ET’s balance is more leveraged relative to its close peers – and the pipeline sector has a higher leverage to start with as a group relative to the overall economy. As seen in the last chart below, ET’s leverage ratios are noticeably above peers like KMI and EPD, either measured in debt-to-equity ratio (135% vs. EPD’s 112% and KMI’s 102%) or debt-to-capital ratio (57.5% vs. EPD and KMI’s ~49%).

Before closing, let me also bring the latest insider transactions to your attention. Two insider transactions were disclosed in November 2025 as shown in the next chart. Director Warren Kelcy’s disclosures revealed two more batches of purchase: Each with 1 million shares at a share price of $16.81 and $16.95, respectively. These insider purchases are of interest for potential investors for at least two reasons, in my view. First, they were made recently at prices close to the current market price. Second, they stand in stark contrast to the insider activities in the broader market (which is dominated by selling).

To sum up, this revisit to ET stock is mostly triggered by three developments since my last writing: The latest disclosures from professional fund managers, the suspension of its Lake Charles project, and also the new guidance for growth capex budget. We have benefited in the past by analyzing position changes from supervisors and insiders, and that’s why our service Envision Early Retirement monitors the movement. In the case of ET, I consider super-investors’ holding prices of ~$17 as a good indication of the stock’s well-balanced return/risk ratio. With the company’s latest capital allocation updates, I see the stock as a solid hold, providing a well-rounded package of reasonable value, generous current yield, and moderate growth prospects.

data.title: Capgemini: Not Expensive, But Not Impressed By WNS Acquisition
data.description:
Capgemini SE (CAPMF)(CGEMY) is continuing to see pretty slow sales cycles as in our previous coverage, in part due to pressure from macro in some markets like France (down almost 5% in sales) but also just more generally. While they are pushing harder into the GenAI stuff, and are trying to consolidate their Agentic AI offering also with the acquisition of WNS, we aren't really thrilled by the business proposition at this point. For the coming Q4, there isn't an acceleration expected and growth is going to be mostly inorganic from the inclusion of WNS. The growth isn't good in absolute terms, we don't think there will be that much more to monetise in GenAI at the moment, and we also don't like what they paid for WNS which is in a similar boat to Capgemini as far as we can see. A pass.

Latest Results

There was a sequential improvement in sales driven by financial services and TMT. Manufacturing is in decline but less so YoY, reflected also in the segment breakdown of operations and engineering revenue only up 1.3%. Q3 revenues are up 2.9% ahead of bookings at 1.5%, better than the 0.2% YoY growth as of the FY but also with a 1% contribution from M&A.

The GenAI and Agentic AI opportunity is not too bad. It's around 8% of bookings at this point which clearly means that it's been holding the fort while macro pressure, the main culprit of the spending pressure on clients, limits overall growth.

In terms of the outlook, things aren't great. They do not expect a better demand environment. Q3 was a solid improvement of a couple of points in growth, but it will not be accelerating beyond that point. Q4 should be in line with Q3 on an organic basis, with additional growth coming from the WNS acquisition.

WNS Acquisition And Bottom Line

We aren't impressed by the acquisition. WNS has not been producing great revenue growth on its own, about in line with Capgemini. Yet with Capgemini trading below 10x EV/EBIT, the acquisition multiple on negligible net financial debt was around 14x. It's a higher margin business, but its cash generation is also worse even as a proportion of revenue.

We don't really see much of a benefit here. While GenAI and Agentic AI solutions are lifting in the bookings mix, we aren't sure of the sustainability of these things as revenue drivers. Of course, the company highlights achievements in physical AI and things like that, but it's not clear how much further these solutions will go and how effective monetising them will be. Moreover, there might be some cannibalisation of its AI solutions from its other personnel-based solutions in its markets.

The main thing lending itself to the Capgemini case is the FCFY. As a standalone, it was producing a little under 10% which is a strong yield. It's quite cheap in absolute terms. But the market is likely not missing this, which means it's going to be about the earnings direction tied to the performance of the AI-based revenues. Markets will likely want to see progress there, which is not to be expected in Q4.
